attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman is admitted to hospital with a perforated peptic ulcer. An emergency laparotomy and oversewing of the perforation is performed. Prior to admission she was in reasonably good health. She has a medical history of cardiac ischaemia. Her medications include metoprolol and irbesartan.

She is making a steady post-operative recovery and during routine physical examination on the second post-operative day is found to have crackles at both lung bases and pitting oedema up to her knees. An intravenous infusion of isotonic saline is running at 40 ml/hr and her urine output over the last six hours has been 120 ml.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
Given her medical history, bibasal crackles, and pitting oedema, this patient most certainly has postoperative fluid overload. Postoperative fluid replacement is aimed at proper organ perfusion. However, deciding how much fluid replacement a patient needs is in inexact science and a few patients may end up having lower than optimal fluid replacement or fluid overload. Patients with fluid overload end up having buildup of fluid within the lungs that can lead to severe respiratory distress. Other complications of postoperative fluid overload include cardiac failure, delayed wound healing, and impairment of bowel function. This patient has bibasal crackles (suggestive of fluid accumulation within the lungs) and pitting oedema of the legs. Whilst she currently does not have any symptoms, this problem must be corrected promptly. The recommended management is administration of IV loop diuretics (eg, furosemide) to bring the fluid volume within the body back to normal.

Not changing the course of management will lead to further accumulation of fluid and cause several complications of fluid overload. Fluid overload is associated with increased mortality, hence it should be treated at once.

Reducing the rate of intravenous infusion will not have any impact. Reducing the rate is a passive approach and not fast enough to avoid complications. Patients with fluid overload require active removal of excess fluids with the help of diuretics.

Digoxin may be used in acute decompensated heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. This patient is currently asymptomatic with no symptoms of congestion or hypoperfusion. She does, however, have signs of congestion and should be initially treated with diuretics to reverse her fluid overload.

Oxygen therapy may be required in patients with respiratory distress due to fluid accumulation. However, this patient is currently asymptomatic and oxygen therapy alone would not treat her underlying condition (ie, fluid overload).
learningPoints: A diuretic should be considered as part of the initial treatment of a patient who is fluid overloaded.
references: Claure-Del Granado R, Mehta R. Fluid overload in the ICU: evaluation and management. BMC Nephrol. 2016;17(1). Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27484681/
